title: Intro to JavaScript
summary: Client-side JavaScript in the browser: adding scripts to HTML, statements, DOM output, comments, var/let/const, expressions and operators, if/else, loops, and strings.

examples.title: What is JavaScript, and how to Include JS in HTML
examples.description: JavaScript runs in the browser (client-side) and makes pages interactive. Put your code in a <script> element—usually just before </body> so HTML loads first (see <!-- Js code below --> in the HTML panel). Every page that needs clicks, live updates, or forms. Change the message in the script or click the button.

files.html:
JavaScript in the browser

JavaScript runs client-side in your browser—alongside HTML and CSS on every modern website.

This line is updated when the <script> below runs.
files.css:
body { font-family: system-ui, sans-serif; padding: 1.5rem; max-width: 36rem; line-height: 1.6; }
button { margin-top: 0.75rem; padding: 0.45rem 0.9rem; cursor: pointer; }

examples.title: JavaScript statements
examples.description: A statement is one instruction JavaScript runs—often one line ending with a semicolon (;). Several statements run in order from top to bottom. Every script is a list of statements. Add a third statement that updates the footer text.

files.html:
Statements demo

Line 1: waiting…

Line 2: waiting…
files.css:
body { font-family: sans-serif; padding: 1.5rem; }
p { margin: 0.35rem 0; color: #475569; }

examples.title: Display output: innerText, textContent, and innerHTML
examples.description: innerText and textContent set plain text (Hello world!). innerHTML renders HTML (world in bold, or an image). textContent/innerText for safe text; innerHTML for markup. Change plain or html.

files.html:

innerText:

textContent:

innerHTML:

Image

innerText / textContent:

innerHTML:

examples.title: JavaScript comments
examples.description: Comments explain code for humans; the browser ignores them. Use // for one line and /* */ for several lines. Document tricky logic or temporarily disable code while debugging. Uncomment the last line in the script to see a different message.

files.html: Run the example to see the result.

examples.title: var, let, and const
examples.description: const holds a value you will not reassign; let is block-scoped and can change; var is older and function-scoped (avoid in new code). let fixes the loop bug where var shares one i. Prefer const by default, then let when you need to reassign. Change let to var in the loop and read the output difference in the page.

files.html:
const, let, and var

    Open the output panel — console.log also shows the loop values.
files.css:
body { font-family: sans-serif; padding: 1.5rem; line-height: 1.6; }
.note { color: #64748b; font-size: 0.9rem; }

examples.title: Expressions and operators
examples.description: An expression produces a value (e.g. 2 + 3). Operators do math (+, -, *, /, %), compare (===, !==), combine conditions (&&, ||), and update variables (+=). Calculations, scores, and deciding if a user is logged in. Change the numbers and see how the results update.

files.html: Operators
files.css:
body { font-family: sans-serif; padding: 1.5rem; }
li { margin: 0.35rem 0; }

examples.title: If / else
examples.description: if / else runs different code depending on a condition—for example a different greeting by time of day. Show welcome-back vs first-visit messages, or enable buttons only when a form is valid. Add an Afternoon button with another message.

files.html: Click a button below.
files.css:
body { font-family: sans-serif; padding: 1.5rem; }
button { margin-right: 0.5rem; padding: 0.4rem 0.75rem; cursor: pointer; }

examples.title: Loops
examples.description: Loops repeat code—for loops count with an index; while loops run until a condition is false. Lists, tables, or repeating console output. Change the loop to count to 10, or rewrite it as while (i <= 5).

files.html: Click Run example — output appears in the panel on the right (like DevTools console).

examples.title: Strings
examples.description: Strings hold text. Use quotes, .length, + to join, template literals (`Hello ${name}`) for embedded values, and methods like .toUpperCase(). Names, messages, labels, and building HTML from data. Add .toLowerCase() on the email part or change the template literal.

files.html: String tools

practice.description: Use a loop to log the multiples of 3 from 3 to 15 in the console output.

starter.html: Click Run to see console output below.
